titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the W.D.G. Batten papers AD.MC.088
titlestmt.author: Brad Bauer

publicationstmt.publisher: NYU Abu Dhabi, Archives and Special Collections

address.addressline: New York University Abu Dhabi, C-2

profiledesc.creation: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on 2026-04-30 15:16:07 +0400.
profiledesc.langusage: Description is written in: English, Latin script.

repository.corpname: NYU Abu Dhabi, Archives and Special Collections

did.abstract: The W.D.G. Batten papers consist of diaries (appointment calendars), correspondence, documents, and ephemera, related to the career of a British officer from the Indian Army, who served in various capacities with the Arab Bureau, as acting Political Agent in Jeddah, and with the political staff of the Egyptian Expeditionary Force in the years immediately following World War I.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions governing access
accessrestrict.p: The W.D.G. Batten papers are open and available for use in the Archives and Special Collections department of the New York University Abu Dhabi Library.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The collection is arranged in one series, with folders arranged in alphabetic order by titles supplied by the processing archivist.

bioghist.head: Biographical note
bioghist.p: W.D.G. (William Douglas Grant) Batten (1890-1934) was born on 9 July 1890 Calicut, India, the son of John Kaye and Eve Muriel Batten. His father worked as a Civil Service in British-administered India, as a judicial commissioner, and census records show the family alternately residing in Britain and in India during the first decades of Batten's life.

In 1913, he was commissioned into the British Army at the rank of Second Lieutenant, after having completed officers training at Cambridge University, and was to serve in India while waiting to be accepted into the Indian Army, which he was the following year, in the 3rd Gurkha Rifles (Queen Alexandra's Own). He was promoted to the rank of Lieutenant in March 1914 and to Captain in September 1915. Little is known about his service during this time, including how he was transferred to the Arab Bureau in the latter stages of World War I. In 1920, however, he was awarded the Order of the Star of Nepal, 3rd class, from the Maharahjadhiraja of Nepal.

Although his diaries and correspondence show that he was active in various capacities, including as a political agent, in the Arab Bureau and the headquarters of the Egyptian Expeditionary Forces, shuttling between Cairo, Jeddah, Jaffa, and Amman, his request to stay in the region was declined by the Colonial Office in 1922, citing a failure to abide by specific regulations of the Indian Army. As a result, after returning to England in May 1922, he returned to India in the latter part of that year.

Public records accessed online show that he was married twice: once in Calcutta in 1914, and again in Surrey in 1921. W.D.G. Batten died in a hospital in Calcutta on 28 October 1934, and a memorial marker was added in the cemetary in his mother's hometown of Nairn, Scotland.

prefercite.head: Preferred citation
prefercite.p: [item], [folder number], W.D.G. Batten papers, MC-088, New York University Abu Dhabi Library, Abu Dhabi, UAE.

scopecontent.head: Scope and contents
scopecontent.p: The W.D.G. Batten papers consist of diaries (appointment calendars), correspondence, documents, and ephemera, related to the career of a British officer from the Indian Army, who served in various capacities with the Arab Bureau in Palestine and Transjordan, as acting Political Agent in Jeddah, and with the political staff of the Egyptian Expeditionary Force in the years immediately following World War I.

The Batten papers are fragmentary in scope, focused chiefly on three pocket diaries (appointment calendars) for the years 1919, 1920 and 1922, as well as individual letters, notes, and printed certificates by which one can trace some of his activities during this time period. The diaries usually contain very brief notes, documenting who he met with on specific days, where he traveled to, and occasionally, who he sent letters or memoranda to, as well as more personal notes about activities, inventories of belongings, and other related items. During this period, the diaries show him traveling back and forth from Palestine to Egypt (in 1918-1920), with a longer period spent in Jeddah in the latter half of 1920, with numerous references to having met with the Hashemite King Hussein, and his sons Ali, Abdullah, and Faisal. The 1922 diary, recorded mostly when he was based in Amman, Jordan, include notes of meetings with H.St.John Philby, Gertrude Bell, Prince Abdullah, and others. Also included from this period is a manuscript brevet, in Arabic, from Hussein, awarding Batten the Order of al Nahda for his efforts assisting the Hashemites to maintain the independence of Hejaz.

Other items include documents relating to his commissioning as a Second Lieutenant in the British Army, with orders to serve in India pending his acceptance into the Indian Army, in 1913, correspondence from various British government ministries, related to his appointments and services, including a letter from the office of the Undersecretary of State in the Colonial Office, Winston Churchill, in 1922, denying Batten's wish to remain in Transjordan, performing work in the political agency. This was due to perceived irregularities in fulfilling his requirements as an officer in the Indian Army, which resulted in the order to return to India. Another printed document, in Nepali, shows that Batten was granted the Order of Nepal, dated 1 Vaisakh 1977 (or April 13, 1920), for his service as an army officer.

userestrict.head: Conditions governing use
userestrict.p: Given the age of these documents, it is presumed that the contents of this collection no longer protected by copyright and are in the public domain. However, it is still the responsibility of researchers to fully investigate the rights status of these materials before quoting from them in a published work, for any uses that exceed those that would be considered as "fair-use."

controlaccess.geogname: Great Britain -- Foreign relations -- Middle East

did.unittitle: Certificate: Brevet bestowed upon Batten, for the order of Al Nahda, from King Hussein ibn 'Ali
Certificate: receipt of the Order of the Nepal Star [certificate in Nepali]
Correspondence: Curzon, George (Lord Curzon); paraphrase copy of telegram text
Correspondence: Foreign Office and Colonial Office
Correspondence: India Office (regarding service as German translator)
Ephemera: fragments of translated Arabic text, and dinner menu card
Ephemera: Hijaz Railway, Staff Duty Pass
Invitations to lunch and dinner with Lord and Lady Milner, from Mr. and Mrs. H. St.John Philby, Amman
